text:
HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD — REASSESSMENT NOTE
Post-Neoadjuvant Restaging Conference

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB/AGE: 66 years old (recently turned 67)
ECOG Performance Status: 1
Attending Surgeon (HPB): [Attending Surgeon]
Attending Medical Oncologist: [Attending Oncologist]
Attending Radiation Oncologist: [Attending Radiation Oncologist]
Attending Radiologist (Pancreatic Protocol Imaging Review): [Attending Radiologist]
Disciplines Present: HPB Surgery, Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Diagnostic Radiology, Pathology, Palliative Care (available), Nutrition

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR TUMOR BOARD REASSESSMENT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 66/67-year-old retired pipe fitter with KRAS G12V-mutated, TP53-mutated, SMAD4-deleted, MSS, HRD-negative borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (initial staging cT3N1M0, Stage III per AJCC 8th edition) who has now completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX. He is presented today for formal multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment to determine appropriateness and timing for surgical resection versus additional locoregional consolidation prior to surgery.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
At initial presentation, Mr. Kowalski was found to have a 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas on pancreatic protocol CT with upstream pancreatic ductal dilation. The tumor abutted the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees of circumference and demonstrated short-segment contact with the SMV-portal vein (PV) confluence, meeting NCCN/AHPBA criteria for borderline resectability. Periportal lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm were suspicious for nodal involvement. Initial serum CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. EUS-guided FNA confirmed pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. PET/CT confirmed no distant metastatic disease. Molecular profiling demonstrated KRAS G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W (VAF 38%), homozygous SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion — notably no HRD (BRCA1/2, ATM, PALB2 all wild-type), MSS, TMB 3.2 mut/Mb. CEA was mildly elevated at 4.8 ng/mL. Biliary obstruction was managed with ERCP and plastic biliary stent placement prior to chemotherapy initiation.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board consensus, the patient was enrolled in a neoadjuvant strategy with FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus + 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) every 2 weeks. Prophylactic pegfilgrastim was administered each cycle. He has now completed all 8 planned cycles.

The course of treatment has been notable for cumulative toxicities. During cycles 1–2, he experienced grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin-induced), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1–2 nausea, and grade 2 diarrhea managed with loperamide and dietary modifications. No febrile neutropenia occurred; ANC nadir was approximately 1.2 x 10^9/L. He lost approximately 3 kg over the initial 4 weeks. Mid-treatment restaging after cycle 4 showed the tumor had decreased from 3.8 to 3.1 cm with reduced SMA abutment and declining CA 19-9 at 380 U/mL; the decision was made to continue FOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles with oxaliplatin dose reduced by 20% (to 68 mg/m2) due to neuropathy accumulation. During cycles 5–8, peripheral sensory neuropathy progressed to grade 2–3 with bilateral hand and foot numbness/tingling and mild fine motor impairment. Cycle 7 was complicated by grade 3 diarrhea requiring a 2-day hospitalization for IV hydration and electrolyte repletion; irinotecan was subsequently dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 (75% dose). One episode of grade 2 oral mucositis occurred. Total weight loss from baseline to present is approximately 7 kg. Pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon) was initiated for exocrine insufficiency. Glycemic control worsened in the setting of chemotherapy; metformin was held on treatment days.

The post-cycle-8 pancreatic protocol CT demonstrates further reduction of the primary tumor to 2.4 cm with meaningful improvement in SMA-tumor relationship and normalization of the periportal lymph nodes. CA 19-9 has fallen dramatically to 85 U/mL (from 1200 U/mL at baseline), a 93% reduction. The patient is now presenting for formal reassessment of surgical candidacy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: 7 kg total weight loss over the course of neoadjuvant therapy; fatigue grade 2, improved somewhat over the past 2 weeks since completing cycle 8. No new fevers. No chills.
Gastrointestinal: Steatorrhea managed with Creon; bowel habits now near-baseline. Mild epigastric discomfort intermittently. No jaundice (biliary stent functional). Good oral intake, tolerating soft diet.
Neurological: Ongoing bilateral foot and hand numbness/tingling (predominant in feet); able to ambulate independently. Mild difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirts). No falls.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. No joint symptoms.
Dermatologic: Mild alopecia (grade 1, stable).
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No dyspnea on exertion. Hypertension: home blood pressure readings acceptable per patient.
Endocrine: Blood glucose control challenging; currently managing with dietary adjustments and periodic insulin correction.
Other systems: Reviewed and negative for significant new complaints.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg; Heart rate 74 bpm, regular; Temperature 37.0°C; SpO2 97% on room air; Weight 77 kg (from 84 kg at baseline, 7 kg total weight loss).
General: Alert, oriented x3. Appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Well-groomed. ECOG Performance Status 1.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcerations active at this time.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the epigastric region and right upper quadrant. No rebound tenderness. No guarding. No palpable abdominal mass appreciated on exam. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds present and normal in all quadrants. No ascites on percussion.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No cyanosis.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Symmetric strength in all extremities. Diminished sensation to light touch in bilateral feet (stocking distribution) and mild diminution in bilateral fingertips. Gait slightly cautious but stable and independent. Romberg test mildly positive.
Skin: Mild scalp alopecia. No rashes. No hand-foot syndrome currently.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY AND TUMOR MARKER DATA:
CA 19-9: 85 U/mL (baseline 1200 U/mL; after cycle 4: 380 U/mL; now 85 U/mL — 93% total reduction from baseline)
CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (baseline 4.8 ng/mL, now normalizing)
CBC: WBC 5.8 x 10^9/L; Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable); Platelets 198 x 10^9/L; ANC 3.2 x 10^9/L
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L; Potassium 4.0 mEq/L; Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60); Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (improved from 2.1 at diagnosis — biliary stent functional); ALT 32 U/L; AST 28 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L; Albumin 3.5 g/dL
Fasting glucose: 148 mg/dL; HbA1c: 7.9% (worsened glycemic control in setting of chemotherapy)
Coagulation: INR 1.1; PT within normal limits
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (mildly low; oral supplementation ongoing)

---

IMAGING REVIEW (Post-Cycle 8 Pancreatic Protocol CT, reviewed by HPB radiologist at tumor board):
The most recent pancreatic protocol CT was reviewed in detail by the attending HPB radiologist at today's tumor board meeting. The findings are summarized as follows:
- Primary tumor: The pancreatic head mass now measures 2.4 cm in greatest dimension, down from 3.8 cm at diagnosis and 3.1 cm at mid-treatment restaging. This represents a meaningful and progressive shrinkage of the primary over the full course of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX.
- SMA involvement: The radiologist notes that the SMA fat plane appears substantially improved relative to baseline. The tumor-to-SMA contact, which originally involved approximately 120–150 degrees of circumference, now appears to involve less than 90 degrees, with partial restoration of the fat plane between the tumor and the artery. The radiologist notes a thin plane of fat visible along the right lateral border of the SMA that was not present at baseline — a favorable finding suggesting possible downstaging of arterial involvement.
- SMV/portal vein confluence: Short-segment contact with the SMV remains visible, though the degree of encasement appears reduced and the vein remains patent without gross occlusion. There is no obvious collateral venous formation. The radiologist's interpretation is that the SMV relationship has improved and that segmental venous resection/reconstruction may still be required intraoperatively but that true encasement appears resolved.
- Regional lymph nodes: The previously suspicious periportal lymph nodes have continued to shrink; the largest now measures 0.6 cm, within normal size range. There is no new pathologic adenopathy identified.
- Distant disease: No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No pulmonary disease on the included lung bases.
- Treatment effect: There is heterogeneity within the primary tumor on the arterial phase, with a region of low attenuation centrally, which the radiologist interprets as consistent with partial tumor necrosis or fibrosis — a favorable marker of treatment response.
- The radiologist's overall interpretation: Significant response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with downsizing and improved vascular relationships. Based on imaging alone, the tumor may now be categorized as potentially resectable, though definitive determination of resectability will depend on intraoperative findings.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION AND ASSESSMENT:

Medical Oncology perspective: Mr. Kowalski has had an outstanding response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX. The CA 19-9 decline from 1200 to 85 U/mL — a 93% reduction — is among the most favorable prognostic markers we can see in this disease. Primary tumor downsizing from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm and normalization of regional lymph nodes are highly encouraging. His molecular profile (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4 deletion, CDKN2A deletion, MSS, HRD-negative) placed him in a biologically unfavorable subgroup at diagnosis; SMAD4 loss in particular is associated with higher risk of systemic failure, yet he has had no distant progression. The HRD-negative status means PARP inhibitors would not be appropriate as maintenance in the adjuvant setting, and the MSS status means checkpoint immunotherapy would not be expected to benefit him. His response has been achieved solely with cytotoxic chemotherapy, which is gratifying. Oxaliplatin-based therapy has been completed and should not be resumed given cumulative neuropathy (currently grade 2–3); any future regimen should be oxaliplatin-free.

HPB Surgery perspective: Based on the radiographic and biochemical response documented, the surgical team believes that resection is now feasible with a reasonable expectation of achieving R0 (negative margin) resection. The improved SMA fat plane on post-cycle-8 imaging is particularly important — arterial involvement was our primary concern at baseline. The SMV will likely require intraoperative assessment and possibly short-segment resection with primary reconstruction, but this is a well-established maneuver at our institution performed routinely. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck and retroperitoneal margins will be critical. The patient's ECOG PS 1 and adequate nutritional status make him an appropriate surgical candidate. We recommend a planned approach with vascular surgery on standby for portal vein/SMV reconstruction if needed.

Radiation Oncology perspective: Before proceeding directly to surgery, the radiation oncology team recommends a period of consolidative chemoradiation. The rationale is to provide further locoregional therapy to the vascular margin zones — particularly the SMA margin, which remains the most challenging zone for achieving adequate clearance in pancreatic head tumors — prior to surgery. A standard long-course approach using IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) over approximately 5.5 weeks with concurrent capecitabine (Xeloda) as a radiosensitizer is recommended. This approach is supported by institutional experience and is consistent with the sequencing used in several cooperative group neoadjuvant protocols. Capecitabine dosing of 825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days is standard. The radiation field would include the gross tumor volume, the operative bed, and regional at-risk nodal stations (celiac axis, SMA, portal/SMV). We would plan to restage with pancreatic protocol CT approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation before proceeding with surgical scheduling.

Radiology perspective (imaging-based downstaging): The radiologist confirms that the imaging findings are consistent with significant response and likely downstaging. Importantly, while the tumor has clearly shrunk and the vascular anatomy appears more favorable, we should not interpret this as meaning the tumor is microscopically negative at the vascular margin — desmoplastic reaction and treatment-related fibrosis can obscure the true extent of residual viable tumor at the margins. This is a well-known limitation of post-treatment imaging in pancreatic adenocarcinoma and underscores the importance of careful surgical technique and intraoperative frozen sections.

Nutrition: The patient has lost 7 kg from baseline, with some of this being pre-diagnosis and some treatment-related. Albumin is 3.5 g/dL. He is on pancreatic enzyme replacement. Oral nutritional supplementation should continue throughout chemoradiation; if oral intake deteriorates during radiation, early enteral support via nasojejunal tube should be considered. Close nutritional monitoring will be important preoperatively.

Palliative Care: Not formally engaged at this visit. Patient maintains ECOG PS 1 and goals of care remain curative in intent. Palliative care remains available for symptom management.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. Borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (initial cT3N1M0, KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative) with excellent biochemical and radiographic response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles.
   - CA 19-9: 1200 → 85 U/mL (93% reduction)
   - Tumor size: 3.8 → 2.4 cm
   - Improved vascular relationships on imaging; SMA abutment < 90 degrees, restored partial fat plane; SMV contact without encasement
   - Regional lymph nodes normalized in size
   - No distant metastatic disease
2. Cumulative oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, currently grade 2–3 (predominant in bilateral feet). Oxaliplatin-based treatment now complete.
3. Treatment-related weight loss 7 kg from baseline; nutritional status suboptimal but manageable; pancreatic exocrine insufficiency on enzyme replacement.
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus with worsening glycemic control in setting of chemotherapy; metformin held.
5. Hypertension, stable.

---

PLAN:
1. CONSOLIDATIVE CHEMORADIATION prior to surgical resection:
   - Proceed with long-course intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT): 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions over approximately 5.5 weeks.
   - Concurrent radiosensitizing chemotherapy: capecitabine (Xeloda) 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days.
   - Radiation planning to include gross tumor, pancreatic head/uncinate, and regional nodal stations at risk (celiac, SMA, portal/SMV).
   - Organs at risk constraints per AAPM TG-101 guidelines (duodenum, stomach, small bowel, kidneys, spinal cord, liver).
   - Oncology to monitor for radiation-related GI toxicity; antiemetic and antidiarrheal supportive measures pre-prescribed.
   - Dose reduction of capecitabine to 750 mg/m2 BID should be available given prior history of GI toxicity.

2. RESTAGING IMAGING after completion of chemoradiation:
   - Pancreatic protocol CT abdomen/pelvis approximately 6 weeks following the last day of radiation.
   - CA 19-9 at time of restaging imaging.
   - Case to be represented to HPB tumor board following restaging to confirm surgical planning.

3. SURGICAL PLANNING:
   - Assuming no evidence of distant progression on restaging imaging, proceed with pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) — open approach preferred given anticipated periportal/perivascular fibrosis from neoadjuvant therapy.
   - Vascular surgery to be notified and available on standby intraoperatively for SMV/portal vein reconstruction if required.
   - Preoperative cardiac and pulmonary clearance to be obtained prior to surgical scheduling.
   - Anesthesia pre-assessment to be arranged.
   - Patient will require biliary stent exchange or upgrade to metal stent if the interval between current stent placement and surgery becomes extended.

4. NEUROPATHY MANAGEMENT:
   - Peripheral neuropathy grade 2–3 from cumulative oxaliplatin; no further oxaliplatin to be administered.
   - Referral to neurology for symptomatic management (consideration of duloxetine, gabapentin, or other neuropathic agents).
   - Physical therapy referral for balance and fall prevention given sensory deficits.

5. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:
   - Continue Creon (pancreatic enzyme replacement) at current dosing with all meals and snacks.
   - Dietitian consultation during chemoradiation to monitor caloric intake and weight.
   - Oral high-calorie/high-protein supplements continued.
   - If oral intake falls below adequate levels during radiation, place nasojejunal tube for supplemental enteral nutrition.

6. GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT:
   - Continue metformin hold per chemotherapy protocol.
   - Endocrinology co-management for insulin-requiring diabetes during chemoradiation; blood glucose logs to be reviewed at each visit.
   - HbA1c recheck at restaging visit.

7. GERMLINE TESTING:
   - Germline BRCA1/2 and ATM testing was recommended at initial tumor board and remains pending — confirm results obtained or reorder if outstanding. Results will not change current curative-intent management but are important for family counseling and future maintenance therapy considerations.

8. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Patient to establish care with radiation oncology this week for simulation and planning.
   - Weekly on-treatment visits during chemoradiation with medical oncology and/or radiation oncology.
   - CA 19-9 to be checked at start of radiation and at restaging.
   - Next HPB tumor board presentation: following completion of chemoradiation and post-treatment restaging.

Patient and his family were present at the conclusion of tumor board deliberations. The multidisciplinary recommendations were explained in detail, including the rationale for adding chemoradiation before surgery, the anticipated surgical procedure and its associated risks (including pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying, bleeding, biliary leak, and the specific requirement for potential vascular reconstruction), and the expectation that if surgery proceeds without distant failure, it offers the best chance for long-term disease control. Patient expressed understanding and agreement with the proposed treatment plan. He remains motivated and engaged in his care. Palliative care was offered proactively; patient declined at this time but was informed of its availability.

Electronically signed:
[Attending Medical Oncologist], MD — Medical Oncology
[Attending Surgeon], MD — Hepatopancreatobiliary Surgery
[Attending Radiation Oncologist], MD — Radiation Oncology
For the Hepatopancreatobiliary Multidisciplinary Tumor Board
